title: Monkeypox Molluscum Contagiosum and Other Poxvirus Infections
authors: Faisal Syed Minhaj, Christina L. Hutson

items.subtopic: Monkeypox, Molluscum Contagiosum, and Other Poxvirus Infections | DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY
items.title: Understanding the Genomic Structure of Poxviruses
items.content: Poxviruses are characterized by a conserved double-stranded DNA genome. The central portion of this genome can be up to 200 kb and encodes essential open reading frames (ORFs) for replication or packaging of virions. Variations in ORF complements across different genera contribute to differences in disease manifestations and host range.
keyPoints: Poxviruses have a conserved double-stranded DNA genome.
The central portion of the genome can be up to 200 kb.
Variations in ORFs are responsible for differences in disease manifestations and virus host range.
items.reference: Monkeypox Molluscum Contagiosum and Other Poxvirus Infections · Monkeypox, Molluscum Contagiosum, and Other Poxvirus Infections | DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Monkeypox, Molluscum Contagiosum, and Other Poxvirus Infections | DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY
items.title: Identifying Human-Infecting Genera of Poxviruses
items.content: Four genera of poxviruses (Orthopoxvirus, Parapoxvirus, Yatapoxvirus, and Molluscipoxvirus) include species that can infect humans. Additionally, there is an unclassified poxvirus known to cause human illness.
keyPoints: Four genera of poxviruses can infect humans: Orthopoxvirus, Parapoxvirus, Yatapoxvirus, and Molluscipoxvirus.
An unclassified poxvirus has also been reported to cause human illness.
items.reference: Monkeypox Molluscum Contagiosum and Other Poxvirus Infections · Monkeypox, Molluscum Contagiosum, and Other Poxvirus Infections | DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY

items.title: Transmission Modes of Orthopoxviruses
items.content: Orthopoxviruses, including variola and monkeypox viruses (MPXV), are primarily spread through direct contact. However, variola virus can also be transmitted via respiratory secretions, with a rare instance of aerosol transmission reported in Germany.
keyPoints: Direct contact is the primary mode for most poxviruses.
Variola virus can transmit via respiratory secretions and possibly aerosols (rare case).
Monkeypox virus has two clades: Clade I endemic to Central Africa, Clade II endemic to West Africa, with subclade IIb spreading globally in 2022.
items.reference: Monkeypox Molluscum Contagiosum and Other Poxvirus Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Geographical and Clinical Distinctions of MPXV Clades
items.content: Monkeypox virus (MPXV) has two distinct clades with different geographical distributions and clinical severities. Clade I is more severe than Clade II, which further divides into subclades IIa and IIb.
keyPoints: Clade I (Congo Basin) causes more severe disease than Clade II.
Clade II endemic to West Africa; subdivided into clades IIa and IIb.
Clade IIb spread globally in 2022, primarily affecting MSM.
items.reference: Monkeypox Molluscum Contagiosum and Other Poxvirus Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Epidemiology of Molluscum Contagiosum Virus (MCV)
items.content: Molluscum contagiosum virus (MCV) spreads through direct contact and percutaneous exposure, commonly seen in pediatrics through play activities. In adults, it can also manifest with genital involvement due to sexual transmission.
keyPoints: MCV spreads via direct contact and percutaneous exposure.
Commonly transmitted among children during play.
In adults, MCV often involves the genitals due to sexual exposure.
items.reference: Monkeypox Molluscum Contagiosum and Other Poxvirus Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Systemic Spread and Disease Severity in Orthopoxvirus Infections
items.content: Orthopoxvirus infections involve systemic spread from the inoculation site to lymph nodes, followed by viremia and skin tropism. The severity is influenced by the innate immune response's ability to control initial infection stages. Immunocompromised individuals experience more severe manifestations.
keyPoints: Systemic spread occurs from the inoculation site to lymph nodes and then develops into symptomatic (febrile) viremia with viral skin tropism.
Disease severity is influenced by the innate immune and interferon responses.

Immunocompromised individuals, such as those with CD4 T cell count <200 cells/mm³ or organ transplant recipients, can experience severe systemic manifestations including wide dissemination of rash and prolonged lesion persistence.
items.reference: Monkeypox Molluscum Contagiosum and Other Poxvirus Infections · ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.title: Lesion Progression and Infectious Period in Orthopoxvirus Infections
items.content: In individuals with intact immune systems, lesions appear about 1 week post-exposure and progress through stages over 7–14 days. The infectious period ends once all scabs separate from the skin and new intact skin forms.
keyPoints: Lesions develop approximately 1 week after exposure in immunocompetent individuals.
Lesion progression occurs over 7–14 days, followed by scabbing.
The infectious period concludes when all lesions have scabbed over, separated from the skin, and new intact skin has formed.
items.reference: Monkeypox Molluscum Contagiosum and Other Poxvirus Infections · ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.title: Distinguishing Features of Smallpox and Mpox
items.content: Smallpox and mpox have similar incubation periods up to 17 days, with fever as the initial symptom followed by a rash. A key differentiator is lymphadenopathy, which is present in mpox but absent in smallpox.
keyPoints: Incubation period: up to 17 days
Initial clinical sign: fever
Rash onset follows after days
Lymphadenopathy presence: key differentiator (mpox has it, smallpox does not)
items.reference: Monkeypox Molluscum Contagiosum and Other Poxvirus Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.title: Clinical Presentation of Clade IIb Mpox
items.content: During the clade IIb outbreak, mpox often presented with rash as the first symptom or concurrently without prodromal symptoms. Lesions were typically localized to the anogenital region and fewer in number (<10), often at different stages.
keyPoints: Rash can be the first sign
Lesions localized primarily in the anogenital region
Fewer lesions, often <10
Lesions may appear in different stages at the same site
items.reference: Monkeypox Molluscum Contagiosum and Other Poxvirus Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.title: Severe Manifestations of Mpox
items.content: Severe mpox manifestations include ocular infection, neurologic complications, myopericarditis, and uncontrolled viral replication in severely immunocompromised patients. These can lead to organ dysfunction and prolonged disease.
keyPoints: Ocular infections: pain, redness, vision changes
Neurologic complications: encephalitis, myelitis
Myopericarditis symptoms: shortness of breath, palpitations
Severe immunocompromise: CD4 <50 cells/mm³ associated with higher mortality
items.reference: Monkeypox Molluscum Contagiosum and Other Poxvirus Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.title: Identifying Poxvirus Infections: Key Differential Diagnoses and Diagnostic Tools
items.content: When evaluating a patient with nodular or vesiculopustular lesions, consider the differential diagnoses of poxvirus infections such as mpox, varicella, yaws, papillomavirus infection, herpes simplex virus, and cutaneous anthrax. The history should include travel, occupation, animal exposures, lesion evolution, sexual history, and symptom timing relative to rash onset. Laboratory testing is crucial for confirmation.
keyPoints: Differential diagnoses include varicella, yaws, papillomavirus infection, herpes simplex virus, cutaneous anthrax, and STIs like gonorrhea, chlamydia, or syphilis.
Up to 10% of patients with mpox may be coinfected with an STI.
Nucleic acid testing (PCR and sequencing) is the most common laboratory tool for diagnosing poxvirus infections.
During the 2022 mpox outbreak, PCR assays specific to non-variola orthopoxvirus (NVO), orthopoxvirus generic, generic mpox, and mpox clade II became widely available.
items.reference: Monkeypox Molluscum Contagiosum and Other Poxvirus Infections · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Laboratory Confirmation of Poxvirus Infections: Techniques and Challenges
items.content: Confirming a poxvirus infection requires laboratory testing due to the similarity in clinical presentation with other conditions. PCR is the primary diagnostic tool, expanded during the 2022 mpox outbreak. Electron microscopy can identify virus particles, while serologic assays are limited by broad antigenic similarity among orthopoxviruses.
keyPoints: PCR and sequencing are essential for identifying poxvirus infections, including new viruses like Akhmeta, Borealpox, and NY-014.
Electron microscopy reveals large, brick-shaped particles for orthopoxviruses; parapoxviruses show ovoid structures with crisscross spicules.
Serologic assays can demonstrate orthopoxvirus reactivity but often cannot distinguish between species due to antigenic similarity.
items.reference: Monkeypox Molluscum Contagiosum and Other Poxvirus Infections · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Tecovirimat Use in Severe Orthopoxvirus Infections
items.content: Tecovirimat, a virustatic agent inhibiting viral egress protein, is the drug of choice for severe orthopoxviral infections. Its use requires optimized immune function for favorable outcomes and should be reserved for severe cases due to potential resistance.
keyPoints: Tecovirimat is dosed at 600 mg orally or 200 mg intravenously every 12 hours, typically for 2 weeks.
A fatty meal enhances enteral bioavailability of tecovirimat.
Resistance can develop due to a single-point amino acid change in the viral target; resistance does not consistently correlate with phenotypic resistance.
Tecovirimat should be reserved for severe infections, especially during smallpox incidents.
items.reference: Monkeypox Molluscum Contagiosum and Other Poxvirus Infections · Poxvirus

items.title: Brincidofovir and Cidofovir in Severe Poxviral Infections
items.content: Brincidofovir (oral prodrug of cidofovir) can be used with tecovirimat for severe poxviral infections. It has a higher resistance barrier, making it less likely to develop resistance.
keyPoints: Brincidofovir is dosed at 200 mg weekly for two doses.
Cidofovir is administered intravenously at 5 mg/kg weekly with concurrent probenecid.
Combination therapy of tecovirimat and cidofovir/brincidofovir may be synergistic in severe infections.
items.reference: Monkeypox Molluscum Contagiosum and Other Poxvirus Infections · Poxvirus

items.title: Vaccinia Immune Globulin (VIG) for Severe Mpox
items.content: VIG, licensed for adverse reactions to smallpox vaccine, is used for severe mpox in immunocompromised patients. Its efficacy in this context remains unclear.
keyPoints: Standard VIG dose is 6000 U/kg intravenously; doses up to 9000 U/kg can be used.
VIG is primarily used when a robust immune response cannot clear the virus.
items.reference: Monkeypox Molluscum Contagiosum and Other Poxvirus Infections · Poxvirus

items.title: Mortality Rates in Poxvirus Infections
items.content: In immunocompetent individuals, most poxvirus infections are self-limited. However, generalized orthopoxvirus infections like MPXV and variola virus have higher mortality rates. Smallpox has a mortality rate of up to 30% in unvaccinated persons, while MPXV clade I, IIa, and IIb have mortality rates of 1.4–10%, 1%, and <1%, respectively. Immunocompromised hosts face more severe infections with higher mortality or atypical presentations.
keyPoints: Most poxvirus infections in immunocompetent hosts are self-limited, resolving in weeks to months.
Generalized orthopoxvirus infections have higher case-fatality rates.
Smallpox mortality rate: up to 30% in unvaccinated individuals.
MPXV clade I mortality rate: 1.4–10%
MPXV clade IIa mortality rate: 1%
MPXV clade IIb mortality rate: <1%
Immunocompromised hosts may experience severe or atypical presentations with higher mortality.
Effective antiretroviral therapy is crucial for favorable outcomes in patients with advanced HIV.
items.reference: Monkeypox Molluscum Contagiosum and Other Poxvirus Infections · PROGNOSIS | ☐ PREVENTION

items.title: Vaccination and Prevention Strategies for Poxvirus Infections
items.content: ACAM2000, a live replicating vaccinia virus vaccine, is administered as a single percutaneous dose. JYNNEOS, a live nonreplicating modified vaccinia Ankara-Bavarian Nordic (MVA-BN) vaccine, requires two subcutaneous doses 28 days apart. Awareness of occupational risks and adherence to barrier precautions prevent most poxvirus infections. Pre-exposure vaccination is recommended for those at risk of occupational exposure.
keyPoints: ACAM2000: live replicating vaccinia virus vaccine, single percutaneous dose.
JYNNEOS: live nonreplicating MVA-BN vaccine, two subcutaneous doses 28 days apart.
Prevention includes awareness of occupational risks and barrier precautions.
Pre-exposure vaccination is recommended for individuals at risk of occupational exposure.
items.reference: Monkeypox Molluscum Contagiosum and Other Poxvirus Infections · PROGNOSIS | ☐ PREVENTION

items.subtopic: Monkeypox, Molluscum Contagiosum, and Other Poxvirus Infections | DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY
items.question: Which part of the poxvirus genome encodes genes with predicted functions in immune evasion?
options: The central portion of the genome
The left end of the genome
The right end of the genome
items.explanation: The left and right ends of the poxvirus genome encode genes with predicted functions in immune evasion.

items.explanation: Four genera of poxviruses (Orthopoxvirus, Parapoxvirus, Yatapoxvirus, and Molluscipoxvirus) include species that can infect humans.

items.question: What is the genomic structure of poxviruses generally conserved across?
items.explanation: The genomic structure of poxviruses is generally conserved across subfamilies, genera, and species.

items.question: Which clade of Monkeypox virus (MPXV) is associated with a higher proportion of severe disease?
items.explanation: Clade I MPXV causes a higher proportion of individuals with severe disease than Clade II.
items.reference: Monkeypox Molluscum Contagiosum and Other Poxvirus Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: What was the primary mode of transmission for Monkeypox virus historically?
items.explanation: Spread to humans is primarily through direct contact (e.g., handling during food preparation) with infected animals.
items.reference: Monkeypox Molluscum Contagiosum and Other Poxvirus Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: Which group has been disproportionately affected by the global spread of clade IIb mpox in 2022?
options: Gay, bisexual, and other men who have sex with men (MSM)
items.explanation: In 2022, clade IIb mpox spread globally, affecting predominantly gay, bisexual, and other MSM.
items.reference: Monkeypox Molluscum Contagiosum and Other Poxvirus Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: What is the primary mode of transmission for Molluscum contagiosum virus (MCV) in adults?
items.explanation: In adults, MCV often transmits through sexual exposure.
items.reference: Monkeypox Molluscum Contagiosum and Other Poxvirus Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: What is the typical timeline for lesion progression in individuals with intact immune systems after exposure to orthopoxvirus?
options: Lesions appear immediately and resolve within a week.
Lesions develop about 1 week post-exposure, progress over 7–14 days, scab, and resolve by 21 days.
Lesions take several weeks to develop but resolve quickly once they appear.
items.explanation: Individuals with intact immune systems typically see lesions about 1 week after exposure, progressing over the next 7–14 days and resolving by 21 days post-rash onset.
items.reference: Monkeypox Molluscum Contagiosum and Other Poxvirus Infections · ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.question: In which condition is systemic spread of MPXV unchecked, leading to severe manifestations?
options: Severe immunocompromised states (e.g., advanced HIV with CD4 T cell count <200 cells/mm³).
items.explanation: In severe immunocompromised states, such as advanced HIV with a low CD4 T cell count, systemic spread of MPXV is unchecked, leading to severe manifestations.
items.reference: Monkeypox Molluscum Contagiosum and Other Poxvirus Infections · ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.question: When does the infectious period for orthopoxvirus end?
options: After all lesions scab over and separate from the skin with newly formed intact skin.
items.explanation: The infectious period ends once all lesions have scabbed, separated from the skin, and new intact skin has formed.
items.reference: Monkeypox Molluscum Contagiosum and Other Poxvirus Infections · ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.question: Which of the following is a key differentiating factor between smallpox and mpox during the prodromal stage?
items.explanation: Lymphadenopathy is present in mpox but absent in smallpox during the prodromal stage.
items.reference: Monkeypox Molluscum Contagiosum and Other Poxvirus Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.question: During the clade IIb outbreak of mpox, how were lesions typically distributed?
options: Diffuse and well-circumscribed with a centrifugal distribution
Localized primarily in the anogenital region
Uniformly distributed across all body parts
items.explanation: Lesions were frequently localized, primarily in the anogenital region during the clade IIb outbreak.
items.reference: Monkeypox Molluscum Contagiosum and Other Poxvirus Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.question: Which of the following is a rare severe manifestation of mpox?
items.explanation: Myopericarditis has been reported as a rare severe manifestation of mpox.
items.reference: Monkeypox Molluscum Contagiosum and Other Poxvirus Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.question: What is the typical duration for lesions in molluscum contagiosum to regress after maturation?
items.explanation: Lesions of molluscum contagiosum gradually regress after 6–12 months.
items.reference: Monkeypox Molluscum Contagiosum and Other Poxvirus Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.question: Which group is at greater risk of poxvirus exposure due to their occupation?
options: Laboratory workers working with poxviruses, farmers, hunters, and sex workers
items.explanation: The passage states that greater risk is in laboratory workers working with poxviruses, farmers, hunters, and sex workers.
items.reference: Monkeypox Molluscum Contagiosum and Other Poxvirus Infections · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a differential diagnosis for poxvirus infections?
items.explanation: The passage lists varicella, yaws, and papillomavirus as differential diagnoses but does not mention influenza.
items.reference: Monkeypox Molluscum Contagiosum and Other Poxvirus Infections · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which laboratory tool is most commonly used for the diagnosis of poxvirus infection?
items.explanation: The passage indicates that nucleic acid (molecular) testing is the most common laboratory tool for diagnosing poxvirus infection.
items.reference: Monkeypox Molluscum Contagiosum and Other Poxvirus Infections · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.question: What is the primary role of tecovirimat in the treatment of orthopoxvirus infections?
options: It acts as a virustatic agent by inhibiting viral egress protein.
It provides immediate immunity against poxviruses.
It serves as a vaccine for smallpox prevention.
It is used to treat bacterial superinfections in orthopoxvirus infections.
items.explanation: Tecovirimat inhibits viral egress protein, preventing cell-to-cell dissemination of mature virions and acts as a virustatic agent.
items.reference: Monkeypox Molluscum Contagiosum and Other Poxvirus Infections · Poxvirus

items.question: Why is immune system optimization crucial for recovery from orthopoxvirus infections?
options: Because antiviral agents provide complete immunity.
As both antiviral agents are virustatic, an immune response is essential to recovery.
To prevent the need for supportive care measures.
It ensures immediate clearance of the virus without treatment.
items.explanation: Both approved antiviral agents against orthopoxviruses are virustatic, necessitating an immune response for recovery.
items.reference: Monkeypox Molluscum Contagiosum and Other Poxvirus Infections · Poxvirus

items.question: What is a key aspect of managing mpox in individuals coinfected with HIV?
options: Initiation, continuation, or reinitiation of antiretroviral therapy.
Using tecovirimat as the sole treatment.
Administering topical cidofovir for all lesions.
items.explanation: Management includes initiation, continuation, or reinitiation of antiretroviral therapy due to its correlation with hospitalization and severity in HIV-coinfected individuals.
items.reference: Monkeypox Molluscum Contagiosum and Other Poxvirus Infections · Poxvirus

items.question: What is the recommended dosing schedule for tecovirimat during severe orthopoxvirus infections?
options: 600 mg orally or 200 mg intravenously every 24 h.
600 mg orally or 200 mg intravenously every 12 h, typically for 2 weeks.
300 mg orally twice daily for 4 weeks.
400 mg intravenously every 8 h for 1 week.
items.explanation: Tecovirimat is dosed at 600 mg orally or 200 mg intravenously every 12 hours, typically for 2 weeks.
items.reference: Monkeypox Molluscum Contagiosum and Other Poxvirus Infections · Poxvirus

items.question: Which poxvirus infection has the highest case-fatality rate in unvaccinated individuals?
items.explanation: Smallpox carries a mortality of up to 30% in unvaccinated individuals.
items.reference: Monkeypox Molluscum Contagiosum and Other Poxvirus Infections · PROGNOSIS | ☐ PREVENTION

items.question: What is the recommended vaccination strategy for individuals at risk of occupational exposure to poxviruses?
options: Two subcutaneous doses of JYNNEOS 28 days apart
No vaccination needed if barrier precautions are used
items.explanation: Pre-exposure vaccination with two subcutaneous doses of JYNNEOS is recommended for those at risk.
items.reference: Monkeypox Molluscum Contagiosum and Other Poxvirus Infections · PROGNOSIS | ☐ PREVENTION

items.question: In immunocompromised hosts, which condition can result in higher mortality due to orthopoxvirus infections?
items.explanation: Immunocompromised hosts may experience severe conditions like progressive vaccinia and eczema vaccinatum, leading to higher mortality.
items.reference: Monkeypox Molluscum Contagiosum and Other Poxvirus Infections · PROGNOSIS | ☐ PREVENTION

items.assertion: Severe mpox manifestations are more common in patients without severe immunocompromise.
items.reason: Most severe cases occur in severely immunocompromised individuals, such as those with advanced HIV or organ transplantation.

items.assertion: Molluscum contagiosum lesions typically cause significant inflammation.
items.reason: Lesions of molluscum contagiosum have little surrounding inflammation and are painless.

items.assertion: Tecovirimat should be reserved for severe orthopoxvirus infections.

items.assertion: Combination therapy with cidofovir or brincidofovir and tecovirimat can be considered in severe infections.
items.reason: Limited animal data suggest that brincidofovir may be synergistic with tecovirimat in orthopoxvirus disease.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, and the reason supports considering combination therapy.
